speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you were getting ready. You knew that you could no longer stay in the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that hiding, period, there or anywhere else, was just not for me. I-- I was terrified. I was so terrified that I didn't even remember the hiding place in her room. It never occurred to me even to crawl in there. I just was frozen, and I knew I could not just wait for them to get me, because every time a doorbell would ring or anytime somebody would knock, I would-- my heart would stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: I needed to be in control of something, or at least I thought. Because this is giving over control, and we sit and wait. When? In half an hour? Tomorrow? A week? It's-- it's a frightful thing. And then these two girls told me that they were going, because they had received-- because I asked them, how do you know that this is OK and this is on the level? And they said-- well, one was very upset that I would even suggest that she wouldn't know what she was doing. She wouldn't hand all that money over to someone, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends of hers went and they had-- from a little town in Belgium and from Brussels-- they'd gotten two cards with a code only she and they knew, like everything is fine, you know, whatever. Weather's great. And these friends heard again from somebody else before that. So this had been going on for a while, so it was absolutely proven.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made out a code. Namely, if they arrived in the first village and in Brussels all right, to send me a card saying, everything is fine. Wright. The code name was Wright. Meanwhile, however, since things were going really, very, very-- a bit dangerous, I must say, more than a bit dangerous. It wasn't safe at all anymore, from one moment to the next. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was engaged to a wonderful man, the young Herbert Nelson, from The Nelson Review, where I worked. He was only half Jewish. His father was full Jewish. His mother was not. And he said that it would perhaps help me a great deal, because I certainly was on their list-- try to get an Aryan father. If somebody could get me an Aryan father, that I'm also only half Jewish, it may save me. And what happened then before, that was right after my arrest, when really, situations started getting a bit dangerous for me. To write-- my mother should write to Budapest to my aunt there, also, of course, in code, that, bottom line, she had an affair when papa was in the First World War. She had an affair with someone there, and out of this affair came I. Well, the first time my fiance suggested that, my mother slapped his face and walked out. So there was no way of her ever writing a letter like this to my aunt, or even suggesting that-- you know, papa would turn in his grave if he knew, and so on. After it was very dangerous for me, she finally decided, you know. I said to her, you know, papa would understand, and this is not about morality. This is about life. She wrote to my aunt, and thank God she did understand what this was all about. And my wonderful, wonderful cousin went out on the hunt, trying to buy me an Aryan father. And while all this took place, I was trying to stay out of trouble through the orphanage and the-- the-- all these things one does, in order not to be caught. When I came home to tell him about the two girls, that they were leaving the same day, my mother was very upset and my fiance was very upset. They didn't want me to leave. They said they were sure I'm going to get these papers. I'm going to get these papers. I'm going to get these papers. To wait. I decided to wait until I heard from them. I got a card from them-- the first card-- about 10 days later, and then again another card. With the code. With the code as we had decided-- Wright. Everything was fine. So I decided to leave. It meant I needed money. I needed jewels. I needed everything. My sister, Kate, had permission to come, and what she did is, when friends heard that I was finally leaving, they brought all their money. They brought all their jewels. They brought everything of value they could have, because they said, bring it to Switzerland. At least we have something after the war. Keep it for us. And my sister, who was very clever in sewing, sewed into the bottom of my coat, into the hemline, into the inside of the pockets, into the inside of the sleeves, truly, truly, a fortune on gems, on jewelry, on foreign currency, on everything you can possibly think. It was a very grey-- I remember a grey, very light cashmere coat-- full. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she came to do this little pocket, you know. Little, little pockets, all of it. And by the time the day was over, I had a treasure chest in front of me. Everybody brought-- everybody brought stuff. My girlfriend, Toni, brought me coins, a lot of foreign coins. Too heavy. I couldn't carry it. And my beloved Margit brought-- said I should bring-- yeah, I should take her guitar, because a girl with a guitar would never be stopped. I don't know why she thought. She said I could either hawk it, you know, get money for it, or I could sing something. Anyway, I got this, and my mother kept running to the post office saying that the papers hadn't come. And I left. I left on the 2nd of May, 1943, on a very beautiful day. I was supposed to meet this Harry. I talked to him on the phone, only Harry. He had my prescription for a fake ID over the phone-- my height, coloring, and so on. I was supposed to be a student living, visiting my grandmother over the weekend. Only one little suitcase, and I met him on the water front. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Strange man. And he took, immediately-- the first thing he wanted was my 2,000 guilders. And then he carried my suitcase. And as we were walking toward the Grand Central Station, where we were supposed to take the train, it occurred to me, a few days ago, I didn't know this man. I didn't even know that he existed. And now, this man was carrying my suitcase, was carrying my money, was carrying my life in his hands. And then we went to the station, and there was a group of other people who-- this station was crazy. Everybody was trying to get out of Amsterdam. It was really, absolutely wild. And there, we met another man, and he was definitely Flemish, a Flemish accent. And then my Harry, the one who was supposed to do the whole thing, disappeared. Never came back. He said, good luck, and disappeared. I did not have tickets to travel. I did not have a permit to travel. I did have the fake ID, except I never had the chance to look at it, because he handed it to me under the table. And I put it in my purse and never looked at it. I didn't know either the name, who I was, where I was going-- anything. And then I was on the train, and nobody was with me. You know, there were so many people. They were just shoving you on. And around me were only Germans. And I was going, had no idea where. The only thing I remember, he said Roosendaal, which is a border-- Holland, Belgium border-- one of them. It was an incredible journey, because I was totally alone. I had no money. I had enough change to buy a sandwich, not enough to go back home, even. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your coat though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was hardly going to undo the coat. I really was not. And there were Germans, Germans, Germans, Germans all over the place. And I know that I went off the-- I went off the pla-- off-- off the train, sat-- sat alone in a station. And trains were coming, and trains were going, and I was totally alone. I had no idea what to do. And then suddenly, somebody came and picked me up and pushed me on another train. And that was the-- the Belgian guy by the name of John. John, yes. One of the people who traveled with us, a beautiful, elderly couple. He always used to say, he's Belgian, you know. He was making the distinction that this would save all the problems in the world. He was a nut, this guy. He was a total nut insofar as he, first of all, he talked only Flemish, which was very hard to understand. And all you-- whatever you questioned him about, he said, it's in the bag. It's in the bag. It's all right. Don't worry about it. It's all all right. We had an elderly couple-- beautiful couple, Dutch couple. A teacher, I would say about 35, and a young couple with a nine-month-old baby. And the young couple, he was a chain smoker, and she was a frightened, frightened little thing. It was one of those girls who from high school, directly goes into the wedding bed. You know, she had no experience with life, anything. The baby was-- would have killed Hitler if he would have known about it, blonde and blue eyed as can be, and beautiful. Very restless, of course, because the mother was very restless. We had to carry-- we had to carry-- It took such a long time. We had to hide in a ditch. Nobody knew why. It took forever to get close to the border. We were hungry. We were cold. We were alone on a road in the middle of the night. And then we came close to the border, and there was, you know, like they have in railroad station, the thing which goes-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the divider, you know. And there was a little guard's house, and there was a German soldier in there. And after we had spent hours in the ditch, we finally were told to line up, one after the other. And I was behind John, and to stop and wait. And John was going in first, and he talked to the soldier. And he put a handful of bills-- not a handful-- it was a whole package, into this guy's hand. And the thing-- the rail-- opened, and we just walked through. And it seemed so ridiculous for us to go through all this baloney to just walk through the gate like nothing. And then we were on Belgian soil. However, Belgium was occupied too. It was also occupied country. Even so, the rumor had it it was much easier there because King Leopold II, who then reigned, had, just before the Nazis-- the Germans-- marched in, burned all the papers. In Europe, you need to register with the police. Not only a foreigner, everybody needs to.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, they know always where you are, what you do, and so forth. There is a national registry where you can find out exactly if you're looking for someone, for $0.50, who, where, what? King Leopold had it all destroyed. There were no papers. In other words, it was much easier for a Belgian Jew to slip through the fingers, at least for a long, long time, than for a Dutch Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why the Dutch didn't do it, because they were so wonderful in many other ways. It was all there. That's how they found everybody. They had it right here. On a little index card. They could find out everybody's name, where they lived, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in Belgium, and we could, they said, go on to Switzerland. And the train ride was absolutely ridiculous. It was like a-- like an adventure film. I mean, they went through so much trouble to get us here, and there, and change trains, and change places, and change-- sleep in a farmhouse, and go somewhere else. It was all incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: We finally ended in Brussels and ended up in a district-- I knew Brussels from before-- I had never been in before. It was a villa district. And there, we spent few days and nights in a fabulous villa with a strange lady whose name we never learned, because she didn't tell us. We called her Madame Zeker. Zeker is the name-- the Dutch name sure, sure. Because whatever we asked her was sure, sure, zeker, zeker. A fantastic villa. I have no idea for whom this lady worked, who paid her, whether this was her villa at all. It was the most magnificent garden you'd ever seen in your life, the most magnificent halls. And then there was, again, crummy bedrooms. So very strange. And John was supposed to bring us papers to get us to Switzerland. We were supposed to get to Switzerland dressed in Red Cross uniforms as doctors and nurses. John didn't come the first night, he didn't come the second night, and all we got from the lady was zeker, zeker. We didn't know. And the third or the fourth night, she woke us and said, [SPEAKING DUTCH]. We've got to go. We've got to go. He's-- They are here-- They are here for us. They are here for us. We've got to go. We didn't know whether we should go in the middle of the night. Yes, no. Yes, no. We did go finally, and outside on the villa, at the entrance gate, was a man we haven't seen before. And as we walked by him he said, my name is Max. My name is Max. Hurry, hurry. We are late. Hurry, hurry. We are late. And then it was pitch dark. We went into a wagon. We didn't know what that was either. Then we decided it was a van, because you could open the doors from the back. We could open the doors from the back. So we were in there, in that van. And they started driving. I have no idea. Pitch dark through Brussels. I mean, so fast, we were thrown about from-- and I knew that on both sides, there were little curtains and windows-- it was very strange-- and benches on the side. And we were looking for Red Cross uniforms, for doctor's uniforms, for nurse's [INAUDIBLE]. There was nothing there, nothing at all. And I don't know whether we drove 15 minutes, 20 minutes. I have no idea. And then, out of suddenly, the doors flew open, and we had lights much stronger than this here, and Germans, Germans, Germans. Raus, raus, raus, raus, raus. And we had driven right into Gestapo headquarters. Avenue-- Avenue Louise, famous Avenue Louise in Brussels. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And dear Max-- who, by the way, I hate to say this, is Jewish, was Jewish-- is the one who delivered us right into Gestapo headquarters. He was part of the whole scheme, this whole elaborate pyramid scheme.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had sent cards, after I was in the little village, to my family. Wonderful. Everything is fine. Come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I had gotten from my friends, as they had gotten from their friends, and so forth, and so forth. And at no time until you were driven into Avenue Louise could you have sensed this whole thing is-- why? What for, all this-- this-- this incredible scheme? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Didn't make sense, did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max was there. He was standing right there, next to the German Nazi. Didn't say a word as we walked by. Much later, I found out about him from people in the first camp in Belgium; that he was, of course, Jewish. They knew him from synagogues, where he was there too, with others. And there was one day when they picked up a lot of people-- a lot of Jews-- he was among them. And while most of the others were never seen again or heard from again, he walked around very free. And as one of the ladies in the camp said, I hope he knows God real good. He has a lot of explaining to do when this is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Max was one of them. And the strange thing is there was a time in the train, shortly before we got to Belgium, when I was sitting there, that a man on a platform, like in a bad movie-- in a trench coat, up, you know, was-- kept making signs to me. And I said, what-- what kind of a thing is this? John was sitting right next to me. He was asleep. Why would he wave me out? And it was-- I didn't know what he wanted. He was definitely making signs to me. I finally woke John and I said, hey, what is this? And he picked me up with all the others, said go, go, go, go. And we ran to other wagons. And later on he said that this guy knows him, and he knows him. They are from the same village, both of them. And this guy knows what John is doing, and this guy, he said, is working for the Nazis. As it turns out, it was just the other way around. This guy wanted to warn us. And of course, who would have believed him? You don't go out with a strange guy making signals to you. You don't go with him. It was exactly the other way around. Why do you think that they sent you the-- the exact signal you wanted? Why didn't they say everything is OK, come, instead of Wright?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. I-- I could pick up whatever I wanted. They didn't tell me what to write. Nobody knew what-- no-- nobody but I, and later say, [? Eva, ?] send a card to you. I say, let's do-- the weather is great. No, no. That was just between you and me. These people did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the women that sent you the cards that said everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. We had made it out between us. They didn't tell me. They didn't tell me what I should-- no, no, no, no. It was not that they said you must write, or I will send you a card with such and such.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write the card after they-- they had gotten you, like they-- they forced you to write a card? Or did you write before the Nazis had you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody, nobody, nobody forced me to write a card. When we crossed over and sp-- spent the first night in a fabulous farmhouse, I can't tell you whether these farmers also knew, whether they were bought or paid for. I don't know. Where we were not wined and dined, but we got incredible food, which we hadn't seen for years already, and stayed that night in the farm house. I don't know who they were. But nobody told us that we had to write or what we had to write. On the contrary, we asked. Come on. We need a card.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We want a card. We want to send the first card from the first village, and then send the next card from Brussels. As a matter of fact, when John did not show the first night, we were very unhappy. We need a card. We want to send them. We got to Brussels. We're staying in a fabulous villa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened then, we were in-- I didn't know where I was, of course. I just know it was not a good place to be. And I somehow got separated from the others. I don't really know where or when. But suddenly, perhaps I looked too long at Max, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But suddenly, I heard the steps behind me, and there was nobody. And I was alone in a courtyard. And at that moment, I could have escaped. There was nobody there. And I didn't ask myself till much later, why didn't I? Well, first of all, I think I didn't want to admit to myself this was not the way to Switzerland. This was number one. Because why not meet a car right there, where they are, in the military or whatever? Would be a fantastic place. Who would look? Who would ask? So I wasn't ready to admit this is not the road to Switzerland. And the second reason was, of course, because if you do not know where you are, you also do not know where to go. Where would I have gone? I had no idea where I was. It was the middle of the night. I was all alone. I did not know Avenue Louise, nor did I know where it was if I would have known it. So I was there until a Flemish soldier, who [INAUDIBLE] spoke French instead of German, which I already couldn't hear anymore, tapped me on my shoulder and led me to-- down in the dungeon somewhere, into a round cell. And the cell-- and he locked the door, and the cell was full-- full, full, full, full, full. I mean really, wall to wall, people standing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody had luggage instead, only us. They were all from Belgium. And they told us where we were. The thing is that, just before they opened the car and brought us out, I tore up my ID in tiny little papers and threw  it through the blinds, out the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want anybody to know where I am, who I am, what. They can tell me what they want. If I have no IDs, I can tell them what I want too. Which I did, by the way. They can't prove anything. Not that I am, not that I am not. As it turned out, it didn't make much sense, because they found it. You wouldn't believe it, what they are looking for. It's-- it's inconceivable. Really, that's all they have to do for. I was called out of the cell, and there was-- and at that time, it worked out that I didn't understand the importance of uniforms, you know, who was higher ranking, whatever. I had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: I only knew-- you know what [INAUDIBLE] are, the [INAUDIBLE]? They had it in German in the student days. It was a sign of honor, when you were fighting with a saber, to have these signs of whatever. So he had a lot of those, and he had a lot of these things here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't know what they meant. And he had, in his hand, these tiny pieces of paper, told me, screaming-- I don't know why they can't talk in a normal voice, but they don't; I think it's to scare you or something like that-- told me that they were found, and that I wasn't as clever as I thought. And I, because I didn't know how important he was, I just didn't care. I told him that this is a lie. It doesn't belong to me anyway, that I was a German actress trying to get to Germany, because in Holland, they didn't like Germans very much anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since it was impossible to get travel permission-- which was true; it took you forever, legitimate-- I went that way. And he said, is that why you crawl with Jews over the border? So I knew then that he wasn't sure that I'm a Jew or not. I'm not. I have nothing. I'm nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me that-- he came back twice. He handed it to me, these little pieces of paper, in an envelope and said that I had to give them to the commandant the next day. And I better, and he's going to spend the rest of his days to find out who I am. And if I lied to him, that I'm going to go straight to hell. Then he locked the cell, and then I was inside that stinking cell and saw nothing but names all over the place. With knife, with scissors, with fingernails-- all sorts of names. And I knew above me there was written in, or scribbled in, may tomorrow never come. And right next to it was [INAUDIBLE], little girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in the cell and then the cell was open-- yes. Then I realized, on the second card, I had no stamp. I had no stamp. What I wanted to do was write, you know, please don't come, something else, to my family. Don't come. It had to stop with me. The-- I had opened the suitcase, and I had in the suitcase-- in this little suitcase-- two bottles of perfume, which I had saved all through the war. One bottle of cologne, [INAUDIBLE], and a bottle of perfume. And that cell was-- stank.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy, the soldier, came in. And he said to me, I should give my bottle for his girlfriend, because where I was going, I didn't need it anymore. So I opened it, and I just poured it out on the floor of the cell. One over me, and one right on the floor of the stones of the cell. And the women came, and they rubbed their hands on their face in it, and it was wonderful. And that stupid guy said to me, well, why would you want to do that for? And he locked the door, and that's when I knew I had no stamp. I need a stamp. And I tried to call him back and say, here's the rest of it. Give me one stamp. But he wouldn't answer the door anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning, we were put on trucks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with soldiers, with guns-- two of them. Two guns on each truck. And driven out of Brussels. And I saw the women, in open truck, take off the jewelry and throw it out on the street. And they told me to do the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't want to do this. They didn't know about that I had much more in here than what I was wearing. I had written on the card in French, will the finder of this card please put a stamp on and mail it? And I threw the card out of the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember I saw it, you know, fluttering, like it couldn't make up its mind where it wanted to land and which way it wanted to land. It was like-- it was really, you know, like-- and then it landed. And my family got it. Weeks later, but they got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this wonderful game ended with me. And then we drove about, I don't know how long. Not very long. Right through gates. It looked like a casern, you know, like a military thing, which turned out to be the first camp I was in, called Malines in French and Mechelen-- M-E-C-H-E-L-E-N-- in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is only, I think, 15 miles or 15 kilometers. I think it's northeast of-- of-- of Brussels. It is between Antwerp and Brussels. It is one of the oldest cities in Europe, totally walled in, not that I saw that ever. I just, you know, afterwards found out. And this is where that famous Belgian lace is coming from, made. This is where it originated, the Belgian-- so this was the best part of Malines, a bet-- the Belgian lace. And then my-- as one of the guys who took me off the truck said to me that, now you are also a guest of the Third Reich. I became a guest of the Third Reich.
